coredata.dc:title: Hybrid SERS platform by adapting both chemical mechanism and electromagnetic mechanism enhancements: SERS of 4-ATP and CV by the mixture with GQDs on hybrid PdAg NPs

coredata.dc:description: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) is a versatile analytical technique widely adapted for the identification of target analytes even at an extremely low concentration. Herein, a unique hybrid SERS platform is demonstrated by utilizing the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hybrid PdAg NPs for the detection of 4-amino-thiophenol (4-ATP) and crystal violet (CV). GQDs offer the rich charge transfer to the LUMO and HOMO of 4-ATP and CV by the CM enhancement. A significant EM enhancement is achieved by the strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of uniquely designed hybrid core-shell PdAg NP, possessing the narrow nano-gaps with the highly dense small Ag NPs and Pd core Ag shell configuration. As a result, orders of enhancement of originally weak 4-ATP and CV Raman signal is witnessed by the combination of electromagnetic mechanism (EM) and chemical mechanism (CM) enhancements on a single SERS substrate. The hybrid GQD/HNP SERS substrate demonstrates significantly improved hot spots and strongly localized electromagnetic fields as confirmed by the finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations. A proper mixture ratio of GQDs and probe molecules presents largely enhanced SERS signals by a strong adsorption of probe molecules through π - π interaction and the enhancement factors vary by the mixture ratio depending on the adsorption kinetics and Raman cross-section of molecules.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of hybrid PdAg nanoparticles (NPs) and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) application for crystal violet (CV) and 4-aminothiophenol (4-ATP). (a) Schematic of SERS application along with the mixture of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and probe molecules. (b) Fabrication steps of hybrid PdAg NP template. (c) Localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of the hybrid PdAg NP template. (d) Schematic of CM and EM enhancements in the SERS process. (e) SERS signals of CV and 4-ATP on the PdAg hybrid NP template.

Evolution of hybrid PdAg NPs by the 5 nm Ag coating on the 20 nm Pd NP templates followed by the annealing between 0, 400 and 600 °C. (a) Schematic presentation of hybrid PdAg NP template fabrication process. (b) – (e) & (b-1) – (e-1) AFM top-views (3 × 3 µm2) and zoom-in top-views (750 × 750 nm2) of hybrid NPs after 5 nm Ag deposition and annealing at various temperatures. (b-3) – (e-3) Cross-sectional line-profiles on the primary NPs. (c-4) – (e-4) Cross-sectional line-profiles on the secondary NPs. (f) Summary plots of Rq and SAR. .

Elemental and optical properties of the hybrid PdAg NPs by the 5 nm Ag coating. (a) – (b-5) SEM image and corresponding EDS maps of Pd, Ag, Al and O. (b) EDS spectrum. (c) – (e) Extinction, reflectance, and transmittance (ERT) spectra in 270 - 1100 nm. The extinction plot is derived from the measured spectra of reflectance and transmittance: extinction (%) = 100% - (reflectance + transmittance)%. (c-1) – (c-2) Corresponding normalized-magnified (NM) spectra within 400 - 700 nm, showing the peak evolution. (c-2) – (e-2) Contour plots of LSPR peak evolution demonstrating a blueshift along with the annealing.

Finite difference time domain (FDTD) simulations of typical Pd NP, Ag NP, core-shelled PdAg NP and hybrid PdAg NP along with their local e-field distribution. (a) – (d) Simulation schematics. (a-1) - (d-1) Top-views of electromagnetic field distribution. (a-2) - (d-2) Side-view distributions.

(a) Raman spectra of (a-1) bare sapphire, (a-2) hybrid PdAg NPs at 600 °C on sapphire, (a-3) 10−7 M of crystal violet (CV) on the hybrid PdAg NPs and (a-4) 2:1 mixture solution of 10−7 M CV and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the hybrid PdAg NPs. (b) Bar diagram comparing the SERS intensity of 4 characteristic peaks. (c) SERS spectra of 10−3 ∼ 10−8 M CV on the hybrid PdAg NPs. (d) SERS intensity comparison of characteristic peaks of CV at different concentrations. The inset presents the prepared CV solution at different concentrations. ± 3% error can be applicable in the Raman intensity measurements.

(a) – (c) SERS signals of mixture ratio variation of CV and GQD at the CV molarity of 10−5 M, 10−6 M and 10−7 M on the hybrid PdAg NPs. 0.25 mg/mL of GQD was utilized. (a-1) – (c-1) Bar diagrams summarizing peak counts of characteristic peaks for the different mixture solutions at different molarity. ± 3% error can be applicable in the Raman intensity measurements.

(a) Raman spectra of GQD layer variation on bare sapphire where a single layer corresponds to the drop-casting of 10 μL of 0.25 mg/mL GQDs. (a-1) Bar diagram summarizing D and G band intensity of GQD layer variation. (b) SERS spectra of GQD layer variation on the hybrid PdAg NPs, where a single layer corresponds to the drop-casting of 4 μL of 0.25 mg/mL GQD. (b-1) Bar diagram summarizing the band intensity of D and G bands of the GQDs. ± 3% error can be applicable in the Raman intensity measurements.

(a) SERS spectra between 10−3 and 10−8 M of 4-aminothiophenol (4-ATP) on the PdAg hybrid NPs (HNPs). (a-1) Bar-diagram showing the peak intensity of four characteristic peaks of 4-ATP with the variation of molarity. Inset shows the prepared 4-ATP solution at various concentrations. (b) Different mixture (10−5 M 4-ATP and 0.25 mgmL−1 GQD solution) ratios of 2:1, 1:1 and 10:1 on the hybrid PdAg NPs. (b-1) Bar diagram showing the enhancement achieved with the mixtures compared to the bare 4-ATP. Inset shows the skeletal view of the 4-ATP molecule. ± 3% error can be applicable in the Raman intensity measurements.

Bar diagram summary of mixture SERS approach with GQD for (a) 4-ATP and (b) CV on the PdAg hybrid NPs (HNPs). (c) Schematic of LSPR on the GQD/HNP combination. (c-1) Top- and side-views of FDTD simulation for the GQD/HNP. (d) Schematic of chemical enhancement (CM) and electromagnetic mechanism (EM) achieved from the charge transfer with the GQDs and HNPs.

head.ce:title: Hybrid SERS platform by adapting both chemical mechanism and electromagnetic mechanism enhancements: SERS of 4-ATP and CV by the mixture with GQDs on hybrid PdAg NPs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) is a versatile analytical technique widely adapted for the identification of target analytes even at an extremely low concentration. Herein, a unique hybrid SERS platform is demonstrated by utilizing the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hybrid PdAg NPs for the detection of 4-amino-thiophenol (4-ATP) and crystal violet (CV). GQDs offer the rich charge transfer to the LUMO and HOMO of 4-ATP and CV by the CM enhancement. A significant EM enhancement is achieved by the strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of uniquely designed hybrid core-shell PdAg NP, possessing the narrow nano-gaps with the highly dense small Ag NPs and Pd core Ag shell configuration. As a result, orders of enhancement of originally weak 4-ATP and CV Raman signal is witnessed by the combination of electromagnetic mechanism (EM) and chemical mechanism (CM) enhancements on a single SERS substrate. The hybrid GQD/HNP SERS substrate demonstrates significantly improved hot spots and strongly localized electromagnetic fields as confirmed by the finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations. A proper mixture ratio of GQDs and probe molecules presents largely enhanced SERS signals by a strong adsorption of probe molecules through π - π interaction and the enhancement factors vary by the mixture ratio depending on the adsorption kinetics and Raman cross-section of molecules.

An innovative SERS platform is demonstrated for the detection of 4-amino-thiophenol (4-ATP) and crystal violet (CV) by the utilization of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hybrid PdAg NPs through the combination of both the EM and CM enhancements.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) has induced extensive research interests due to its extraordinary capability in the detection of Raman signals of target molecules even at tremendously low concentrations. Various applications have been demonstrated in a wide range of fields, i.e., the forensics, clinical diagnosis, food control, environmental regulation, bio-sensor, health, homeland securities etc., just to name only a few [1–4]. Originally weak Raman signals can largely be enhanced by the metallic nanostructures and semiconductor nanomaterials through the electromagnetic mechanism (EM) chemical and mechanism (CM) enhancements [1–4]. Recently, the plasmonic nanoparticles (NPs) stimulated a great deal of interests in the SERS application due to their high surface area, tunable optical properties, surface functionality and simple synthesis [1,2,5,6]. The metallic NPs demonstrate significantly enhanced electromagnetic fields and hot-spots on the NP surfaces as well as in between the nanogaps, collectively known as the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect [7–13]. The LSPR significantly enhances the weak Raman signals by many orders, which is generally referred as the EM enhancement. Various metallic NPs such as Ag, Au, Al, Cu, Pt and Pd have been widely explored [1,4,10]. For example, the Ag nano-cubes on the PDMS film demonstrated a flexible SERS substrate and largely improved localized e-field due to the increased edges and sharp boundaries [14]. Ag NPs can demonstrate high sensitivity and superior stability and thus has been widely adapted for the SERS substrate in various configurations of nanostructures [15]. At the same time, well-designed Pd dendritic nanoparticles exhibited a strong hot-spot generation due to the high surface-to-volume ratio, strengthening the SERS of R6G [16]. The Pd NPs have shown improved catalytic activity with the potential as an active SERS substrate [17] and various morphologies of Pd nanostructures demonstrated beneficial physical and chemical properties. The Pd NPs can be employed in SERS due to their high chemical reactivity whereas the Ag NPs have a significant LSPR response, which can be utilized together to further improve the SERS. In addition, the plasmonic NPs made of noble elements such as Pd and Ag can offer the mechanical and chemical stability with low oxidation in air. Advanced nanoparticle assemblies with the precise control of interparticle spacings in a very close proximity are the essential components in designing of SERS substates to improve the enhancement factors [18].

Meanwhile, the low-dimensional semiconductor nanostructures (LDSN) such as TiO2, ZnO, MoS2, WS2, WO3 and GQDs have attracted increased research attentions in the SERS application due to the distinctive charge transfer capability, which can lead to the large SERS enhancement. The LDSN generally possess large surface area with the ample accessible edges and can thus effectively adsorb the target molecules [19–25]. The LDSN can offer the quantum confinement, tunable band-gap and superior surface functionality, making them suitable for the SERS candidates [1,19–24]. The SERS enhancement by the charge transfer to the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) as well as the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of probe molecules by the semiconductor materials is generally known as the CM enhancement [19–24]. Particularly, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have demonstrated the extraordinary charge transfer capacity along with the low cytotoxicity, chemical stability and superior biocompatibility [24,26]. To this end, a sophistically designed metallic NP template with the combination with GQDs on a single SERS template can be a smart choice to improve SERS signals of target molecules. The combination of EM and CM together on a single SERS substrate would be a valuable attempt for the SERS enhancement.

In this work, a hybrid SERS platform combing the EM and CM enhancement is demonstrated by the incorporation of GQDs and hybrid PdAg NPs for the detection of 4-amino-thiophenol (4-ATP) and crystal violet (CV) as seen in Fig. 1 (a). The hybrid PdAg NP template is fabricated by a dual-phase solid-state dewetting (SSD) approach and demonstrates a unique configuration of Ag-shell Pd-core NPs with the highly dense tiny Ag NPs in the background as seen in Fig. 1(b). It is worthwhile to mention that this kind of highly dense Ag NPs normally cannot be fabricated by a typical approach. The hybrid PdAg NP template offers significantly improved hot spots and strong localized electromagnetic fields as seen in Fig. 1(c) and thus can considerably contribute to the SERS enhancement by the largely improved EM enhancement as seen in Fig. 1(d). The GQD enables an ample charge transfer to the HOMO-LUMO levels of the 4-ATP and CV, contributing to the CM enhancement as seen in Fig. 1(d). As a result, orders of SERS enhancements were observed for both 4-ATP and CV by the combination of LSPR and charge transfer effects as seen in Fig. 1(e). Systematic FDTD simulations are performed for the considered metallic NP and hybrid SERS substrates, confirming significantly improved electromagnetic fields with the increased maximum local e-field intensity. The strategy utilizing both the EM and CM on a single SERS substrate by the combination of GQD and hybrid PdAg NPs can be a useful route to the SERS enhancement.

Double-side polished c-plane sapphire of 430 µm thick wafers with ± 0.1° off-axis (iNexus Inc., South Korea) was adapted as a substrate due to its high temperature and thermal shock resistance [27]. Sapphire shows a broad transmission range from the UV to the mid-IR range and exhibits lower background noise [28]. The sample was degassed in a pulsed laser deposition (PLD) chamber (DaDa TG Corp., South Korea) under 1.0 × 10−4 torr at 600 °C for 30 min. The morphology and optical analyses of bare sapphire are provided in Fig. S1. Two NP templates of Pd and Pt were prepared with the initial film thickness of 20 nm Pd and 10 nm Pt, followed by 800 °C annealing for 450 s, which led to the formation Pd and Pt NP templates based on the solid-state dewetting (SSD) [29]. Then Ag films of 5, 10, and 20 nm were deposited on the NP templates and annealed at 400 and 600 °C for 120 s. The thin film deposition of Pd, Pt, and Ag was performed in a plasma-assisted sputtering chamber with the 99.999% purity targets at the ionization current of 3 mA, the pressure of 1 × 10−1 torr and deposition rate of 0.05 nm s − 1. All annealing processes were performed inside the PLD chamber with a temperature ramping rate of 4 °C s − 1 under 1 × 10−4 torr, which was controlled by the computer recipes to obtain the consistency. The growth process was terminated by shutting down the PLD system and the samples were kept under vacuum until the holder temperature was cooled down to the room.

The 4-aminothiophenol (4-ATP, Sigma Aldrich, United States) and crystal violet (CV, Sigma Aldrich, United States) were dissolved in the ethanol to prepare the 10−3 M of stock solution. Subsequently, the stock solution was diluted up to 10−8 M as necessary. The 0.25 mg/mL of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) solution was prepared by dissolving GQD powders (Sigma Aldrich, United States) in ethanol. Three different mixture solutions (1:1, 2:1; 10:1) with the GQD solution were prepared and 10 μL was drop-casted on the hybrid PdAg NPs to measure the SERS of various mixtures of 4-ATP and CV. The Raman spectra were measured with the Raman spectrometer UNIRAM II (UniNanoTech, South Korea) using a 532 nm laser. In the measurement, the 100 × objective lens was used with the laser spot size less 1 μm on the sample surface. All the Raman signals were uniformly accumulated by 10 times with the slit width of 200 μm and grating of 1200 gr./mm.

Non-contact mode atomic force microscope (AFM) (XE-70, Park Systems Corp., South Korea) was adapted for the NP morphology analysis and 3D surface characterization with the same batch of tips having a radius of curvature < 10 nm, force constant 40 nm−1, and resonant frequency ∼ 300 kHz. The average roughness (Ra), RMS roughness (Rq), and surface area ratio (SAR) were used for analysing the morphology of NPs. The Ra represents the average roughness calculated at each pixel and the Rq is described by the relation

, where Hn is the profile height at each pixel. The SAR is the representation of a 3D surface area (As) concerning the geometric area (Ag) by the relation SAR =

× 100%. A scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Regulus 8230, HITACHI, Japan) was also adapted for the morphology analysis and an energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscope (EDS) (Noran System 7, Thermo Fisher, United States) was used for the elemental analysis. Optical system (NOST I, Nostoptiks, South Korea) equipped with the ANDOR sr-500i spectrograph, CCD detector, and halogen-deuterium light sources were used for the optical characterizations. For the simulations of Ag, Pd, hybrid, and alloy NPs, finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) (Lumerical Solutions, Canada) was adapted to calculate the electromagnetic (EM) field distributions. The complex refractive indices of Pd, Ag, sapphire and GQD were adapted from the previously reported references [30–32]. More details of simulations can be found in Fig. S9.

Fig. 2 shows the surface morphology and elemental analyses of hybrid PdAg NPs by the deposition of 5 nm Ag coatings on 20 nm Pd template as the NP template development for the SERS application. Initially, the Pd NP template was fabricated in the 1st phase solid-state dewetting (SSD) with the 20 nm Pd film followed by the 800 °C annealing in Fig. 2(a-1) and 2(b) [33,34]. Then, the Ag films of 5, 10, and 20 nm were deposited on the Pd NP templates and annealed at 400 and 600 °C for 120 s for the 2nd phase SSD as seen in Figs. 2(a-2) and 2(a-3). Depending on the Ag coating thickness in the 2nd phase SSD, the resulting samples demonstrated totally different configurations of NP morphologies such as hybrid PdAg NPs in Figs. 2(d) and 2(e) and fully alloyed bimetallic PdAg NPs in Fig. S2. The hybrid PdAg NPs indicate that the primary Pd NP is coated with Ag atoms, making a PdAg core-shelled NP and also very small secondary Ag NPs are formed in the background as seen in Fig. 2. Such high-density Ag NPs of small size normally cannot be formed by a regular SSD approach [33,34]. The alloy PdAg NPs indicate that the Pd and Ag atoms are fully alloyed in the NP geometry as seen in Fig. S2.

The SSD enables the adatom diffusion at a much lower annealing temperature as compared to the melting point of Pd (1555 °C) [33] and as a result, the Pd NP possessed the average height and diameter of 100 and 300 nm respectively as shown in Figs. 2(b-2) and 2(b-3). Now, along with the annealing after the 5 nm Ag coating, there appeared no obvious change as seen in Figs. 2(d) – 2(e). Meanwhile, the irregular NP clusters were fabricated in the alloy PdAg NP set in Fig. S2 due to the significantly enhanced intermixing. In this set, the primary Pd NPs were all retained regardless of the annealing temperature and the size and density of NPs were similar as seen in Figs. 2(d) – 2(e). With the detailed analyses of line-profiles and EDS phase maps, the primary NP size was indeed gradually decreased in Figs. 2(b-3) – 2(e-3) and the secondary Ag NPs were developed in the background as seen in Figs. 2(c-4) – 2(e-4). Along with the Ag NP growth in the background, the effective height of primary NP was gradually decreased as seen in the line-profiles, which was reflected as a gradual size decrease in the Rq and SAR in Fig. 2(f).

The EDS maps clearly showed that the Pd atom distribution was retained at the initial NP site and there was no obvious diffusion of Pd atoms in Fig. 3 (a-2). Ag atoms was found all over the surface with much higher Ag count at the Pd NP site, indicating a preferential Ag adatom diffusion toward the Pd NPs [35]. The original NP template formed in the first phase of SSD was stable and possess the higher chemical potential or lower surface energy. Thus, the Ag adatoms can diffuse towards Pd NPs and resulted in the Ag shelled Pd core NP configuration [35]. At the same time, the small and high-density Ag NPs were formed in the background along with the increased diffusion energy. Meanwhile, the EDS phase maps of fully alloyed PdAg NPs showed a good match between the morphology and elemental phases, indicating well mixed atoms distributions in the NPs due to the enhanced diffusion and intermixing by the thicker Ag film deposition as in Figs. S4 – S7. Figures S11 – S14 show the AFM and EDS data for the hybrid PdAg NPs with the 10 nm Ag coating. The 10 nm Ag coating set also demonstrated a similar growth trend of hybrid NPs with larger background Ag NPs due to the increased amount of Ag adatoms. In addition, 5 and 20 nm Ag coatings on the 10 nm Pt NP templates also demonstrated the similar growth phases and evolution trends in Figs. S15 - S21: i.e., hybrid PtAg NP with the 5 nm Ag coating and alloy PtAg NPs with the 20 nm Ag film.

Figs. 3(c) – 3(e) present the optical properties of hybrid PdAg NPs fabricated with 5 nm Ag coatings. The distinctive intensity, peak and dip positions along with their bandwidth are the result of corresponding morphology variations owing to the formation of hybrid PdAg NPs and the development of Ag NPs in the background [36,37]. The hybrid NPs resulting from the 5 nm deposition of Ag on the Pd NP templates (0 °C) showed a narrower major peak around 510 nm in Fig. 3(c). The 400 and 600 °C samples showed a blue shift in the resonance peak positions toward the shorter wavelength as seen from the normalized extinction spectra in Fig. 3(c-1). This blue shift in the peak positions with the annealing can be due to the decreased size of NPs [38]. The resonance peak bandwidth became narrower with the increased annealing temperature, which can be due to the improved uniformity of NPs with more thermal energy. There was a significant blue shift in the DR peak: i.e., 575 nm of Pd NPs template to 470 nm of hybrid PdAg NPs at 600 °C as clearly seen in Fig. 3(c-2). Also, the intensity of DR peak was increased up to 40% with a narrower bandwidth when coated with Ag. This can be due to the combined effect of the LSPR of Pd template along with the dominant plasmonic response of Ag coating as a shell structure and background Ag NPs. For the alloy set, a gradual redshift was observed along with the temperature due to the gradually increase NP size [39]. The reflectance and transmittance spectra showed in Figs. 3(d) and 3(d). First, the reflectance spectra show the absorption dips corresponding to the QR and DR extinction peaks. The DR extinction dip became narrower at 450 nm in the case of the Pd NPs while the extinction peak had a broader resonance peak. The shoulder peak position was around 650 nm for the 0 °C sample while it was around 500 nm for the 600 °C sample as shown in Fig. 3(d-1). The reflectance spectral peaks showed a similar trend of blue shift and narrowing of peak bandwidth as shown in the contour plot in Fig. 3(d-2). This mismatch in the extinction dips of reflectance spectra and the extinction spectra peak can be due to the backscattering of small NPs [40]. Furthermore, the transmittance, normalized-magnified (NM) spectra and contour plot are shown in Figs. 3(e) – 3(e-2). Generally, the transmittance dips almost correspond to the extinction DR peak for the hybrid PdAg NPs. After deposition of Ag, the dip region at the VIS region was shallow with much lower intensity as compared to the Pd template. With the development of background Ag NPs, the dip region got slightly shallower and blue-shifted.

Fig. 4 presents the finite difference time domain (FDTD) simulations of pure Pd NP, Ag NP, core-shelled PdAg NP and hybrid PdAg NP. The first column shows the schematics for the FDTD simulations in Figs. 4(a) - 4(d) and the corresponding top- and side-views of electromagnetic field distributions are shown in the 2nd and 3rd columns in Figs. 4(a-1) - 4(d-2). Additional simulation results are provided in Fig. S9 including the alloy PdAg NP and GQD/hybrid PdAg NP configuration. The EM hotspots normally occur on the edges of NPs as seen in the top- and side-views of FDTD simulations as shown in Figs. 4 and S9 [40,41]. The maximum local e-field intensity (MLEI or Emax) was 5.4 and 5.0 for the Pd NPs in the top and side-views and 7.4 and 6.2 for the Ag NPs as shown in Figs. 4(a-1) and 4(b-2). Then, the Emax was increased to 9.2 and 9.1 for the alloy PdAg NPs in Fig. S9. The core-shelled NP with the Pd core Ag shell configuration demonstrated 11.31 and 10.54 in the top- and side-views in Fig. 4(c-1) and (c-2). It is worthwhile to mention that the core-shell configuration demonstrated higher maximum local e-field intensity likely due to the dense interface formation at the Pd-core and Ag-shell. Finally, the Emax became 19.7 and 14.3 for the hybrid PdAg NPs in Figs. 4(d-1) and 4(d-2). As compared to the Pd NP, the hybrid PdAg NP demonstrated nearly 3.65 × increase in the maximum local e-field intensity due to the core-shelled configuration and small Ag NPs in the background. The nano-gaps and interfaces were largely increased with the formation of high-density background Ag NPs. Like mentioned, the electromagnetic hotspots typically form on the edges and boundaries of NPs [40,41] and thus the small size Ag NP can significantly contribute to the EM hotspot enhancement. Normally, such high-density Ag NPs of small size cannot be produced by the regular annealing approach [33,34]. As a result, the hybrid PdAg NP is found to be most suitable for the SERS application as it demonstrated significantly increased EM hot spots by the strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and high density of interfaces so that the electromagnetic enhancement of SERS can be effectively adapted.

Fig. 5 shows the Raman and SERS signals measured on the bare sapphire (0001) and hybrid PdAg NP substrate with the 5 nm Ag coating and subsequent annealing at 600 °C. As mentioned, the hybrid NP substrate has a high density of background Ag NPs with small interparticle spacing and thus can provide a greater number of hotspots for the electromagnetic enhancement of Raman signals. Also, the high-density Ag NPs can offer much higher interface cross-sections for the target SERS molecules. The plasmonic NPs can offer the mechanical and chemical stability with low oxidation in ambient and thus NPs can improve the SERS performance with stability [42]. The bare sapphire showed the Raman peak at 750 cm−1 in Fig. 5(a-1), which corresponds to the characteristic Eg phonon mode of c-plane sapphire [43]. The PdAg NPs on the sapphire showed no Raman signals by itself in Fig. 5(a-2) [43]. The 10 μL 10−7 M crystal violet (CV) drop-casted on the hybrid PdAg NP substrate showed several peaks as presented in Fig. 5(a-3). The band at 913 cm−1 is from the ring skeletal vibrations and bands at 1614, 1579, and 1533 cm−1 can be attributed to the ring CC stretching vibration of CV [44,45]. Other peaks such as 1169, 760, and 727 cm−1 are corresponded to ring CH bends. The 804 cm−1 band is due to the phenyl-H bending, and the band at 1374 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CV [44,45]. The inherently weak Raman signals of CV can be amplified by the electromagnetic field localized in the vicinity of hybrid PdAg NPs creating the hotspots, which is known as the electromagnetic mechanism (EM) enhancement. Furthermore, the mixture of CV (10−7 M) and GQD at the ratio of 2:1 exhibited a significant enhancement in the SERS signal in Fig. 5(b). The existence of GQD provides more carrier supply to the probe molecules, leading to surface-enhanced Raman scattering [46], known as the chemical mechanism (CM) enhancement. To further investigate the ability of SERS substrate of hybrid PdAg NPs, the molarity variation of CV was performed between 10−3 to 10−8 M and the corresponding SERS signal was shown in Fig. 5(c). The increase in molarity from 10−8 M to 10−3 concentration demonstrated a gradual increase in the intensity of the characteristic peaks of CV as shown in Figs. 5(c) – 5(d). The plasmonic hybrid PdAg NP template demonstrated an effective SERS enhancement [46] as clearly seen in Figs. 5(c) – 5(d). Especially, the detection of 10−8 M of CV was clearly demonstrated, indicating the significant Raman enhancement through the EM enhancement by the hybrid PdAg NPs.

Fig. 6 presents an additional investigation on the combined EM and CM SERS substate by the mixture approach (CV:GQD of 10:1, 2:1, and 1:1) on the hybrid PdAg NPs at the different concentrations of CV (10−7, 10−6, and 10−5 M). At different 10−5, 10−6, and 10−7 M concentrations of CV were applied on the hybrid PdAg NPs as shown in Figs. 6(a) - 6(c), respectively. The characteristic peaks of CV at the different concentrations on hybrid PdAg NPs appeared in the SERS spectra. The hybrid PdAg NPs template demonstrates the huge SERS enhancement due to the abundant hot spots induced by strong LSPR. amongst them, CV with the 10−5 M concentration on the hybrid PdAg NPs template shows the highest SERS signal enhancement as shown in Fig. 6(a). Further, the concentration of CV:GQD was varied and applied to the hybrid PdAg NPs template. Generally, the 10:1 mixture demonstrated the highest SERS intensity for each concentration as clearly summarized in the bar diagrams in Figs. 6(a-1) – 6(c-1). The higher CV concentration demonstrated a higher level of enhancement. In other words, the lower GQD concentration demonstrated a higher level of enhancement, which can be likely due to an adequate amount of CV molecules and sufficient charge transfer to the probe molecules. The charge transfer can be assisted via the π - π interaction between GQD and CV and thus increasing adsorption of CV to GQD can contribute to the chemical mechanism (CM) enhancement [47]. Although, this transferred energy can be small as compared to the electromagnetic enhancement, the π - π stacking between GQDs and CV molecule reduces the distance and enhances the charge transfer process. The probe molecules consist of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels, where the charge from the GQDs can easily be transferred to the probe molecules. Thus, the large number of CV molecules may contribute to the enhancement by the charge transfer process. As a result, a higher enhancement was obtained in the 10:1 mixture and the enhanced SERS signal itself may be the direct experimental proof of change transfer to the HOMO and LUMO. An excessive amount of GQDs might block the EM enhancement and block the CV Raman signals for detection.

Fig. 7 presents the Raman of layer-dependant GQD on the bare sapphire and hybrid PdAg NPs. Firstly, the GQD layer variation was performed on the bare sapphire as shown in Fig. 7(a) to exclude the EM enhancement. 10 µl of 0.25 mg/mL of GQD was drop-casted on the bare sapphire substrate, considered as one GQD layer. The D and G peaks correspond to the vibration due to the dangling bonds in the carbon-based materials and vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms, respectively [48]. The D and G peak intensity of GQDs was gradually improved and peak broadening was observed with the additional layers as clearly summarized in Fig. 7(a-1). It can be due to the increased scattering and the reduced inter-defects by the increased GQDs [49]. When the GQD layer (4 μL of 0.25 mg/mL as one layer) was applied on the hybrid PdAg NP substrate, the D/G peaks were also observed, and small satellite peaks were observed as seen in Fig. 7(b). The smaller peaks such as 790, 820, 900 and 945 cm−1 observed in the spectrum could be due to the GQD with the edge defects and the functional groups associated with it. The GQD adsorbed on the hybrid PdAg NP template may gradually increase the intensity of Raman signals. Furthermore, the small peaks observed from 1100 ∼ 1400 cm−1 can be attributed to the edge defect sites. In detail, the peak observed at 1195 and 1285 cm−1 can be assigned to the sp2-sp3 carbon and COOH/COH edge functional groups respectively [50]. The peak intensity gradually became stronger along with the addition of layers. The GQD is the nanoscale piece of graphene having the topological edges defects and the functional molecular groups such as bio-molecules can easily attached to it, whose Raman signal can be enhanced due to the hybrid PdAg NP substrate [51]. The overall Raman intensity of D/G peaks showed similar count as compared to that on the bare sapphire, i.e., the 2-layer (20 μL) GQD on the bare sapphire showed nearly similar count of D/G peaks with 5-layer (20 μL) GQD on the hybrid PdAg NP in Figs. 7(a-1) and 7(c-1). This clearly indicates that the GQD application on the hybrid PdAg NP does not increase the Raman signal of GQD but only the probe molecules can benefit the EM and CM enhancements.

Fig. 8 presents the SERS of 4-aminothiophenol (4-ATP) on the hybrid PdAg NP substrate. The gradual concentration variation of 4-ATP on the hybrid PdAg NP substrate was evaluated between 10−3 M to 10−8 M as seen in Fig. 8(a) and the SERS signal intensity was gradually increased for all characteristic peaks of 4-ATP with the concentration in Fig. 8(a-1). The peaks at 1195 (CH bending), and 1077 (C-S stretching) cm−1 are assigned to the a1 vibration mode [52]. The b2 vibration modes correspond to 1140 (CH bending), 1386 (CH bending + CC stretching), 1435 (CC stretching + CH bending), and 1570 (CC stretching) cm−1 [53]. The SERS signal intensity was gradually increased with the increased concentration of 4-ATP and the highest intensity was observed at 10−3 M. The enhancement can be mainly due to the electromagnetic field enhancements in the vicinity of NPs and electromagnetic hotspots amongst nanogaps of NPs as discussed [46]. In detail, the plasmonic hybrid PdAg NPs can demonstrate the strong LSPR property, which can enhance the generation of hot spots at the edge site. Also, the hybrid PdAg template showed the high density of background NPs, which can enable the generation of hot spots between the small interparticle spacing. These can largely induce the increased EM enhancement and improve the SERS signal for the 4-ATP with high intensity. For the mixture approach, three different mixture solutions (2:1, 1:1, 10:1) of 10−5 M 4-ATP and 0.25 mg/mL GQDs were assessed in Fig. 8(b). In the case of 4-ATP, the 2:1 mixture showed the highest enhancement as clearly seen in Fig. 8(b-1). The 2:1 mixture showed the maximum enhancement of about 10 folds in Fig. 8(b-1) as compared to the 10−5 M SERS spectra on the hybrid PdAg NPs in Fig. 8(a-1). Meanwhile, the enhancement with the 1:1 mixture was about 6 folds and about 2.5 folds for the 10:1 mixture. The charge transfer and adsorption via the π - π interaction between GQD and 4-ATP can be different from the CV [47]. The insert in Fig. 8(b-1) shows the skeleton view of a 4-ATP molecule with the thiol, amino and benzene-electron functional group. The thiol group is broken to form a metal-S bond while the protonated amino group can be adsorbed on the GQD surface through the electrostatic force [54]. A higher concentration of 4-ATP:GQDs might be beneficial for the case of 4-ATP as compared to CV depending on the adsorption kinetics and Raman cross-section of molecules. Finally, the enhancement in the SERS spectra of 4-ATP was effectively achieved by the combined EM and CM enhancement. The EM and CM enhancements were achieved through the increased LSPR of hybrid PdAg NPs, and the enhanced charge transfer due to the strong interaction between 4-ATP and GQDs.

Figs. 9 (a) and 9(b) compare the SERS intensity of 4-ATP and CV mixed with the GQD on the hybrid PdAg NPs. The SERS enhancement factor by GQD for the 4-ATP is summarized in Fig. 9(a) and 9(b. The value in y-axis shows the enhancement ratio of the mixture for the 10−5 M 4-ATP and 10−7 M CV. It can be clearly seen that the proper mixture ratio such as 2:1 provided the highest enhancement with the factor of 10 to 13 times. In the case of CV, the SERS enhancement was better at a low mixing ratio such as 10:1 with a factor of 4 to 4.5 as shown in Fig. 9(b). Here the enhancement is just for the GQDs as we are comparing the SERS on the PdAg NPs and GQD + PdAg NPs. The enhancement factors (EFs) are separately calculated for the PdAg NPs and GQD + PdAg NPs in Tables S8 – S11. Generally, the EF was ∼ 1010 for the 10−7 CV in Table S8 and reached ∼ 1011 with the 10:1 mixture of GQD in Table S9. Similarly, the EF was ∼ 109 for the 10−5 4-ATP in Table S10 and reached ∼ 1010 with the 2:1 mixture of GQD in Table S11. Depending on the probe molecule, different mixture ratios demonstrated the distinct enhancement likely due to the different adsorption kinetics to GQD and Raman cross-section of probe molecules [55]. Finally, in terms of the SERS enhancement mechanisms of 4-ATP and CV, there should be two enhancement factors discussed, i.e., firstly by the hybrid PdAg NPs and then by the GQDs. The enhancement by the hybrid PdAg NPs can be related to the electromagnetic mechanism (EM) enhancement [56] and that of GQDs can be associated to the chemical mechanism (CM) enhancement [47]. As shown in Fig. 9(c), the LSPR of hybrid PdAg NPs and hot-spots of GQD can be excited by the visible photon irradiation. The generated electromagnetic field can promote the formation of electron clouds on the hybrids NPs and electron-hole pairs in the graphene QDs. The electromagnetic fields of GQD/HNP configuration by the FDTD simulation is seen in Fig. 9(c-1). The top- and side-views show the significantly enhanced hot spot generation with much higher intensity of e-field as compared to the bare HNPs. The HNP generally showed the e-field maximum (Emax) of 19.7, which was increased to Emax of 44.1 after the GQD application in the top-view as shown in Fig. 9(c-1). The charge transfer from the conduction band of GQD and from the excited states of hybrid PdAg NPs to the LUMO of probe molecular is described as seen in Fig. 9(d). Initially, the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO can demonstrate the direct resonant Raman scattering when excited by the laser [56]. Additional electrons can be contributed from the metal NPs and GQDs, which can further enhance the Raman signals by the EM and CM enhancements [57,58]. The hotspots generation between the HNPs can be significantly increased along with the GQDs application in the nanogaps. This finally can help the enhancement of Raman signals along with the appropriate amount of GQD application. The GQD can support the abundant charge transfer by the chemical enhancement mechanism (CM) and thus can further enhance the SERS signals of 4-ATP and CV through the π - π interaction.
In summary, a unique hybrid SERS platform is demonstrated for the detection of 4-amino-thiophenol (4-ATP) and crystal violet (CV) by utilizing the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hybrid PdAg NPs.

Distinctive morphologies of PdAg NPs were demonstrated by a dual-phase solid-state dewetting (DSSD) on sapphire (0001). The hybrid PdAg NP configuration, indicating Ag shell and Pd core NPs with the high-density small Ag NPs in the background, was achieved with the thinner Ag coatings, i.e., below 10 nm, and fully alloyed bimetallic PdAg NPs resulted with the thicker Ag films. The hybrid PdAg NPs demonstrated much improved hot spots and strong electromagnetic fields by the strong LSPR due to the unique morphology of Ag shelled Pd core configuration with the high-density Ag NPs. Systematic investigations on the SERS enhancement were performed by the application of various molarities of 4-ATP and CV on the hybrid PdAg NPs and by the mixture ratio variation with the GQDs. The PdAg hybrid NPs demonstrated the strong enhancement of SERS for the 4-ATP and CV, confirming the superior EM enhancement. The GQDs along did not show SERS enhancement on the PdAg hybrid NPs, indicating GQD Raman signal does not amplify by the EM enhancement. Then, the mixture of probe molecules with the GQDs demonstrated further SERS enhancement through the combination of EM and CM enhancements. Overall, 4-ATP and CV demonstrated several orders of SERS improvements by the GQD mixture application on the PdAg hybrid NPs. The enhancement of SERS signal with the mixing technique showed dependency upon the probe molecules, concentration, and mixture ratio with the GQDs depending on the adsorption kinetics and Raman cross-section of molecules.
